ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general tiramisu entry; no specific Japanese-style tiramisu node exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKN covers tiramisu as a cake/pastry category item; no Japanese-specific variant code exists. Closest parent match applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for tiramisu or Japanese-style tiramisu dessert.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Japanese Tiramisu is approximately 120 g, equivalent to one standard dessert slice or individual cup portion as served in Japanese-style cafes and patisseries.

nutrient_source.title: 905D Japanese Tiramisu — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry 905D Japanese Tiramisu with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central tiramisu reference (USDA SR Legacy #19180) as HPB does not disaggregate fatty acid fractions; this is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Japanese Tiramisu in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 62 (medium) based on the closest analogous dessert category — sponge cake / cream-layered cake — from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × carbohydrate_g per serving / 100 = 62 × 29.44 × 1.2 / 100 ≈ 11.0 g per 120 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (18.7 g/100g) with low dietary fibre (0.6 g/100g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention; sodium at 173 mg/100g is moderate but the sugar-fibre imbalance independently elevates blood pressure risk.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit Japanese Tiramisu to very small, infrequent portions and prioritise low-sugar, high-fibre dessert alternatives to avoid insulin-driven sodium retention and arterial stiffness from saturated fat.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 18.7 g/100g with dietary fibre of only 0.6 g/100g produces a rapid postprandial glucose spike; high saturated fat (~14.5 g/100g) from mascarpone and cream further worsens peripheral insulin resistance.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit Japanese Tiramisu; if consumed, restrict to a small portion (≤60 g) paired with a protein-rich food to partially blunt the glycemic response, and monitor blood glucose closely.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat of ~14.5 g/100g and cholesterol of 121 mg/100g from mascarpone, heavy cream, and egg yolks significantly elevate LDL cholesterol and cardiovascular risk when consumed regularly.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should treat Japanese Tiramisu as an occasional indulgence only, keeping portions to ≤80 g and avoiding regular consumption given the high saturated fat and cholesterol load that raises atherogenic LDL.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Japanese Tiramisu is a Japanese-style adaptation of the classic Italian tiramisu dessert, popular in Japanese patisseries, cafes, and as a packaged dessert item sold in Singapore and across Asia. Unlike traditional Italian tiramisu which relies heavily on espresso-soaked savoiardi biscuits and raw egg yolks, the Japanese version typically features a lighter, airier mascarpone or cream cheese mousse layer, sometimes incorporating matcha, hojicha, or yuzu flavours in place of or alongside coffee, resulting in a more delicate flavour profile. The texture is characteristically silky and mousse-like, with a soft sponge or ladyfinger base. Per 100 g, it provides 353 kcal, 23.86 g fat (predominantly saturated from mascarpone and heavy cream, estimated ~14.5 g/100g), 29.44 g carbohydrates, 18.7 g sugar, 5.65 g protein, 0.6 g dietary fibre, and 173 mg sodium. The high sugar and saturated fat content make it a high-concern food for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes and cardiovascular disease. Cholesterol is elevated at 121 mg/100g. It is not suitable for diabetic or heart-disease patients in regular portions. Nutritional data sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry 905D.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Japanese tiramisu?
What is the difference between Japanese tiramisu and Italian tiramisu?
Is Japanese tiramisu high in sugar?
Is Japanese tiramisu suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry 905D Japanese Tiramisu with a confidence of 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy tiramisu reference as HPB does not disaggregate fatty acid fractions. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for cream-layered sponge cake. Overall confidence is high for macros and moderate for fatty acid sub-fractions and GI.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (14.5 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for tiramisu (NDB #19180) as the HPB Foodo database does not report disaggregated fatty acid fractions for this item. This is an adapted value and is flagged accordingly. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this specific Japanese Tiramisu preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 3. GI of 62 is an analogous estimate based on cream-layered sponge cake from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for Japanese Tiramisu exists in any approved GI source. 4. The food is classified as Japanese cuisine as per the input cultural metadata and HPB source record. Multilingual requirement for Japanese dishes (Japanese native-script name) is satisfied by ジャパニーズティラミス. 5. No zero-value core macros are present; zero-macro justification rule (g4) is not triggered.
